questions.question:
The most relevant addressing mode to write position-independent codes is:
A. Direct mode
B. Indirect mode
C. Relative mode
D. Indexed mode
questions.to_find: Which addressing mode enables position-independent code

understand.plain: Position-independent code (PIC) is code that executes correctly regardless of where it is loaded in memory. We need to find which addressing mode achieves this by not depending on absolute memory addresses.

keywords.explain: Code that runs correctly no matter what memory address it is loaded at, without needing modification.
keywords.example: Shared libraries in Linux use PIC so multiple programs can load them at different addresses.

keywords.explain: The effective address is computed as PC + offset. Since the offset is fixed relative to the current instruction, the address adjusts automatically when code is relocated.
keywords.example: If current PC=1000 and offset=10, effective address=1010. If loaded at 2000, same offset gives 2010 — still correct relative position.

keywords.explain: The instruction contains the absolute address of the operand. If code is moved, this address is no longer valid.
keywords.example: MOV [5000], R1 — always accesses address 5000 regardless of where the code is loaded.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='200' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>PC-Relative Addressing</text><rect x='20' y='50' width='120' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>PC = 1000</text><rect x='180' y='50' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='240' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>offset = +20</text><rect x='340' y='50' width='120' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='400' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>EA = 1020</text><line x1='140' y1='70' x2='180' y2='70' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><line x1='300' y1='70' x2='340' y2='70' stroke='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><text x='240' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>If relocated: PC = 2000, offset still +20</text><rect x='340' y='130' width='120' height='40' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='400' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>EA = 2020 ✓</text><text x='240' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Relative offset is self-adjusting — position independent!</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing PC-relative addressing: PC + offset = effective address. When code is relocated, PC changes by the same amount, so EA adjusts automatically.

given.aim: Identify the addressing mode that makes code position-independent.

terms.meaning: Effective address = absolute address in instruction
terms.connects: Not position-independent: absolute address breaks on relocation

terms.meaning: Instruction contains address of a memory cell that holds the actual address
terms.connects: Still uses absolute addresses; not inherently position-independent

terms.meaning: Effective address = PC + signed offset; offset is relative to current instruction
terms.example: BRANCH +10 jumps 10 bytes forward regardless of load address
terms.connects: As code is relocated, PC moves by the same amount; EA stays correct — this IS position-independent

terms.meaning: Effective address = base address + index register
terms.connects: Base still requires absolute or setup; not inherently position-independent

given.plan: Recall that position-independent code must not contain absolute addresses. Only relative (PC-relative) addressing automatically adjusts effective addresses when code is loaded at a different location, because the displacement is always relative to the program counter.

steps.title: Understand requirements for position-independent code
steps.apply: For code to be position-independent, all address references must be computed relative to the current position in memory (PC), not as fixed absolute values. Any mode that embeds absolute addresses fails when the program is relocated.
steps.note: Shared libraries and PIC executables rely on this property.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
Direct mode: EA = absolute address — breaks on relocation.
Indirect mode: EA = Memory[absolute address] — breaks on relocation.
Relative mode: EA = PC + offset — self-adjusting, position independent.
Indexed mode: EA = base + index — base is still absolute unless set up carefully.
steps.note: Relative addressing is the standard technique used by compilers to generate PIC (e.g., -fPIC flag in GCC).

solution.result: C. Relative mode — because effective addresses are computed as PC + offset, they automatically adjust when the code is loaded at any address.

questions.formula_note: PC-relative addressing: EA = PC + displacement. Displacement is a fixed signed offset embedded in the instruction, making the code location-independent.

questions.question:
In the program scheme given below, indicate the instructions containing any operand needing relocation for position-independent behaviour. Justify your answer.

    Y = 10
    MOV  X(R0), R1
    MOV  X, R0
    MOV  2(R0), R1
    MOV  Y(R0), R0
    .
    .
    .
X : WORD  0, 0, 0
questions.answer: MOV X, R0 (instruction 2) — it uses direct/absolute addressing with symbol X, which is an absolute address that must be relocated when the program is loaded at a different address.
questions.to_find: Which instruction(s) in the given program need relocation for position-independent behavior

understand.plain: A program is given with several MOV instructions using different addressing modes. We need to identify which ones use absolute (non-relative) addresses, because those will break if the program is loaded at a different memory address and will need to be patched (relocated).

keywords.meaning: The process of adjusting absolute addresses in a program when it is loaded at a memory address different from where it was compiled.
keywords.example: If X was compiled assuming address 5000 but loaded at 6000, every reference to X must be updated to 6000.

keywords.meaning: EA = Base register + constant offset. The offset is relative, not absolute, so no relocation is needed.
keywords.example: MOV X(R0), R1 means R0 holds a base; X is an offset — if X is a compile-time constant offset (not an absolute address), no relocation needed.

keywords.meaning: The operand or its address is given directly as a constant or symbol that resolves to an absolute address.
keywords.example: MOV X, R0 loads the value at absolute address X into R0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='500' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='500' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='250' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Relocation Analysis</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='300' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>MOV X(R0), R1  — base+offset, X is offset constant</text><rect x='320' y='45' width='80' height='30' class='ok' rx='3'/><text x='360' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>No reloc</text><rect x='10' y='85' width='300' height='30' class='no' rx='3'/><text x='20' y='105' class='sub' fill='white'>MOV X, R0      — direct: X is absolute address!</text><rect x='320' y='85' width='80' height='30' class='no' rx='3'/><text x='360' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>RELOC!</text><rect x='10' y='125' width='300' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='20' y='145' class='sub ink'>MOV 2(R0), R1  — base+literal 2, no symbol</text><rect x='320' y='125' width='80' height='30' class='ok' rx='3'/><text x='360' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>No reloc</text><rect x='10' y='165' width='300' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='20' y='185' class='sub ink'>MOV Y(R0), R0  — Y=10, immediate offset</text><rect x='320' y='165' width='80' height='30' class='ok' rx='3'/><text x='360' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag' fill='white'>No reloc</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing four MOV instructions. Only 'MOV X, R0' uses an absolute address symbol X and needs relocation. The others use register-relative or immediate offsets.

given.aim: Identify which MOV instructions use absolute addresses (need relocation) vs relative addresses (no relocation needed).

terms.meaning: Y is an immediate constant (10), not a memory address
terms.example: MOV Y(R0), R0 means R0 = Memory[R0 + 10], offset is a fixed number
terms.connects: No absolute address involved — no relocation needed

terms.meaning: X is a label in memory (an absolute address) where three words are stored
terms.example: X resolves to some absolute address like 5000 at link/load time
terms.connects: Any instruction that uses X as a direct address will embed that absolute address and need relocation

terms.meaning: X(R0) means R0+X as an offset (X is a displacement constant); X alone means absolute address of X
terms.example: MOV X(R0), R1 — if X is treated as offset (not absolute address), OK; MOV X, R0 — X is the absolute address of the data
terms.connects: MOV X, R0 embeds absolute address of X — must be relocated

terms.meaning: Register-relative addressing with small integer or immediate constant offsets
terms.example: 2(R0): EA = R0+2; Y(R0): EA = R0+10 — both relative, not absolute
terms.connects: No absolute addresses embedded — position independent, no relocation needed

given.plan: For each instruction, determine if any embedded address operand is absolute (symbol that resolves to an absolute memory address). If yes, that instruction needs relocation. Register-relative (base+offset) and immediate operands do not need relocation.

steps.title: Analyze MOV X(R0), R1
steps.apply: Here X is used as a displacement/offset constant added to R0. The absolute address is computed at runtime using R0 (which presumably already points to the data segment). This is base-register + displacement mode — no absolute address is embedded in the instruction itself.
steps.note: If X is simply a numerical offset constant, no relocation needed. But if X is the absolute address of the symbol, it DOES need relocation. In most assembly conventions, X(R0) treats X as an integer displacement — position independent.

steps.title: Analyze MOV X, R0
steps.formula_raw: EA = X (absolute address of symbol X)
steps.apply: This is direct (absolute) addressing. The instruction contains the absolute memory address of symbol X. When the program is loaded at a different address, X's actual address changes, but the embedded address in this instruction does not — it must be patched (relocated).
steps.note: This is the key instruction that requires relocation.

steps.title: Analyze MOV 2(R0), R1 and MOV Y(R0), R0
steps.apply: MOV 2(R0), R1: offset is literal 2 — no symbol, no absolute address. MOV Y(R0), R0: Y=10 is an immediate constant, so EA = R0 + 10. Neither instruction embeds an absolute address.
steps.note: Y is defined as the constant 10 (not a memory address label), so Y(R0) is just R0+10.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Only 'MOV X, R0' uses X as a direct absolute address. This is the instruction that needs relocation for position-independent behavior.
steps.note: All other instructions use register-relative or immediate-constant addressing, which are inherently position-independent.

solution.result: The instruction 'MOV X, R0' (instruction 2) needs relocation because it uses X as an absolute memory address. The other instructions use base-register + offset or immediate operands and are position-independent.

questions.formula_note: Relocation requirement: any instruction that embeds an absolute symbol address in its encoding must be relocated when the program is loaded at a different base address. Register-relative (base+displacement) and immediate operands are self-relative and need no relocation.

questions.question:
Match the pairs in the following questions:

(A) Base addressing        (p) Reentrant
(B) Indexed addressing     (q) Accumulator
(C) Stack addressing       (r) Array
(D) Implied addressing     (s) Position independent

A. A-p, B-r, C-q, D-s
B. A-s, B-r, C-p, D-q
C. A-q, B-r, C-p, D-s
D. A-r, B-p, C-q, D-s
questions.to_find: Correct matching of addressing modes to their characteristics/use cases

understand.plain: Four addressing modes are given on the left and four characteristics or use cases on the right. We need to match each mode to what it is best known for or used for. Understanding the purpose of each addressing mode helps solve this.

keywords.explain: Effective address = base register + offset. When the base register holds the program's load address, all addresses are relative — making code position-independent.
keywords.example: Segmented memory uses base registers; code in a segment is relocatable.

keywords.explain: Effective address = base + index register. Index steps through elements of an array, making it ideal for array traversal.
keywords.example: A[i] = base_address + i * element_size — index register holds i.

keywords.explain: Uses an implicit stack pointer. Push/pop operations use no explicit address operand. Reentrant procedures store local data on the stack, so multiple invocations don't interfere.
keywords.example: Recursive functions use the stack for local variables — each call gets its own frame.

keywords.explain: The operand is implicit in the instruction, typically the accumulator. Instructions like ADD implicitly operate on the accumulator register.
keywords.example: In accumulator-based CPUs, ADD X means Accumulator = Accumulator + Memory[X].

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='480' height='220' fill='var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)' rx='8'/><text x='240' y='28' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Addressing Mode Matching</text><rect x='10' y='45' width='190' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='105' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>A. Base addressing</text><rect x='280' y='45' width='190' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='375' y='67' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(s) Position independent</text><line x1='200' y1='62' x2='280' y2='62' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ga)'/><rect x='10' y='90' width='190' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='105' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>B. Indexed addressing</text><rect x='280' y='90' width='190' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='375' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(r) Array</text><line x1='200' y1='107' x2='280' y2='107' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ga)'/><rect x='10' y='135' width='190' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='105' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>C. Stack addressing</text><rect x='280' y='135' width='190' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='375' y='157' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(p) Reentrant</text><line x1='200' y1='152' x2='280' y2='152' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ga)'/><rect x='10' y='180' width='190' height='35' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='105' y='202' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>D. Implied addressing</text><rect x='280' y='180' width='190' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='375' y='202' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>(q) Accumulator</text><line x1='200' y1='197' x2='280' y2='197' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#ga)'/><defs><marker id='ga' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Four addressing modes matched to their uses: Base->Position independent, Indexed->Array, Stack->Reentrant, Implied->Accumulator.

given.aim: Match each addressing mode to its primary characteristic or use case.

terms.meaning: EA = base register + offset; base holds program start address
terms.example: Used in segmented memory for relocation
terms.connects: Achieves position independence — programs can be loaded anywhere

terms.meaning: EA = base + index; index increments to step through consecutive memory locations
terms.example: Array traversal: base=array start, index=element number
terms.connects: Naturally suited for array access — matches (r)

terms.meaning: Uses implicit stack pointer; PUSH/POP operate without explicit address
terms.example: Function calls save return address and locals on stack
terms.connects: Enables reentrancy — each call has its own stack frame — matches (p)

terms.meaning: Operand is implicitly the accumulator; no address field needed in instruction
terms.example: Early CPUs (8085): ADD B means A = A + B; A is implied

given.plan: Match by understanding the primary purpose and characteristic of each addressing mode: base -> position-independence, indexed -> arrays, stack -> reentrancy, implied -> accumulator.

steps.title: Match Base addressing
steps.apply: Base addressing uses a base register (often holding load address) plus a fixed offset. This makes code relocatable — programs loaded at any address simply load the base register with the new start address. This is the mechanism for position-independent code. Match: A -> s (Position independent).
steps.note: Base registers in x86 segmented model provide exactly this relocation capability.

steps.title: Match Indexed addressing
steps.apply: An index register is incremented to step through successive elements of an array. This is the most natural mode for array traversal. Match: B -> r (Array).
steps.note: Index registers were introduced specifically to avoid rewriting instructions for each array element.

steps.title: Match Stack addressing
steps.apply: Stack-based addressing uses the stack pointer implicitly. Each function call pushes a new frame; local variables and return addresses are stored on the stack. This allows the same function to be called recursively or concurrently (reentrant). Match: C -> p (Reentrant).
steps.note: Reentrancy requires that each invocation has its own data storage — the stack provides this automatically.

steps.title: Match Implied addressing
steps.apply: Implied (or implicit) addressing means the operand is not specified in the instruction — it is always the accumulator. This is characteristic of accumulator-based CPU architectures. Match: D -> q (Accumulator).
steps.note: Early microprocessors like 8085 used implied accumulator addressing for arithmetic operations.

solution.result: B. A-s (Base -> Position independent), B-r (Indexed -> Array), C-p (Stack -> Reentrant), D-q (Implied -> Accumulator)

questions.formula_note: Addressing mode characteristics: Base addressing enables position-independence, indexed addressing enables array traversal, stack addressing enables reentrancy, implied addressing uses the accumulator implicitly.

questions.question:
The instruction format of a CPU is:

Mode and RegR together specify the operand. In particular, Mode = 2 specifies that RegR contains a CPU register and Mode = 2 specifies the address of the operand. An instruction at memory location 2000 specifies Mode = 2 and the RegR refers to program counter (PC).

A. What is the address of the operand?
B. Assuming that it is a no-jump instruction, 2000 specifies Mode = 2 and the RegR is incremented by 1 after fetching the execution of this instruction?
questions.answer: A. The address of the operand is the value of PC after fetching the instruction (PC = 2001, so operand address = 2001). B. The next instruction address is 2002 (PC is incremented to 2001 after fetch, then incremented by 1 more after execution, giving PC = 2002).
questions.to_find: Address of operand and next instruction address when Mode=2 with RegR=PC at instruction location 2000

understand.plain: A CPU has an instruction at address 2000. The Mode field = 2 means the operand address is stored in the specified register (RegR). Here RegR is the PC (program counter). After fetching an instruction at 2000, PC becomes 2001. So the operand address is 2001. Since RegR (PC) is incremented by 1 after execution for a no-jump instruction, PC becomes 2002 — that is the address of the next instruction.

keywords.explain: A mode where the register used as address pointer is automatically incremented after the operand is accessed.
keywords.example: MOV (R0)+, R1 uses R0 as address, then increments R0 — useful for stepping through arrays.

keywords.explain: A special register that holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched. After fetching an instruction at address N, PC becomes N+1 (for word-addressed memory) or N+size.
keywords.example: Instruction at 2000 is fetched; PC is updated to 2001 during/after fetch.

keywords.explain: The register contains the effective address of the operand, not the operand itself.
keywords.example: If Mode=2, RegR=R5, and R5=5000, then operand is at memory address 5000.

id='pa' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/></marker><marker id='ga' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing instruction at 2000, operand at 2001 (where PC points after fetch), and next instruction at 2002 (after PC is auto-incremented post execution).

given.aim: Find (A) the operand address and (B) the next instruction address, given Mode=2 with RegR=PC for instruction at address 2000.

terms.meaning: The instruction is stored at memory location 2000
terms.connects: After fetching, PC is automatically incremented to 2001

terms.meaning: The operand's address is the current value of PC (after fetch)
terms.example: After fetching instruction at 2000, PC = 2001, so EA = 2001

terms.meaning: After the instruction executes, RegR (which is PC) is incremented by 1
terms.example: PC was 2001 after fetch; after execution PC = 2001 + 1 = 2002
terms.connects: Next instruction will be fetched from address 2002

given.plan: Step 1: When instruction at 2000 is fetched, PC advances to 2001. Since Mode=2 means RegR holds the operand address and RegR=PC, operand address = PC = 2001. Step 2: PC is incremented by 1 after execution: PC = 2001 + 1 = 2002. Next instruction is at 2002.

steps.title: Determine operand address (Part A)
steps.apply: The instruction is at address 2000. When the CPU fetches this instruction, PC is incremented to 2001 (standard fetch-increment behavior). Mode=2 means RegR contains the operand's address. RegR = PC = 2001. Therefore, the operand address = 2001.
steps.note: This is auto-increment (or PC-relative deferred) addressing — using PC as a data pointer lets you embed operand values right after the instruction in the instruction stream.

steps.title: Determine next instruction address (Part B)
steps.apply: After fetching, PC = 2001. The problem states RegR (PC) is incremented by 1 after execution. So after execution: PC = 2001 + 1 = 2002. For a no-jump instruction, this means the next instruction is fetched from address 2002.
steps.note: The operand at address 2001 is consumed by this instruction; PC stepping past it (to 2002) is why PC-indirect auto-increment works as an 'immediate' addressing mode in some ISAs (e.g., PDP-11).

solution.result: A. Operand address = 2001 (PC value after fetch). B. Next instruction address = 2002 (PC incremented by 1 after execution).

questions.formula_note: PC auto-increment addressing: when PC is used as a pointer register with post-increment, the operand immediately follows the instruction in memory, and PC advances past it after access — a technique used in PDP-11 ISA for immediate and absolute addressing modes.
